How do annual working hours influence labour productivity across countries over time?

Kale-Ab Bobe

2026-03-29

Introduction

This report attempts to answer following questions:

How do annual working hours influence labour productivity across countries?

Is higher productivity associated with stronger economic growth?

This dataset examines the relationship between labour productivity, annual working hours, and economic growth across multiple countries from 2015 to 2023. The data was sourced from Our World in Data , which compiles global economic indicators from institutions such as the World Bank and OECD.

The dataset includes four variables: Country, annual hours worked, labour productivity (GDP per hour worked), and GDP growth (%). Country is a categorical variable identifying each nation, while the remaining variables are numerical and measure labour input, economic efficiency, and economic performance respectively. Together, these variables enable analysis of how work intensity relates to productivity and overall economic growth.

The dataset contains 54 observations and 6 variables.

# Show structure and first 2 rows
str(data[1:2, ])
## tibble [2 × 6] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)
##  $ Entity                              : chr [1:2] "China" "China"
##  $ Code                                : chr [1:2] "CHN" "CHN"
##  $ Year                                : num [1:2] 2015 2016
##  $ Annual working hours per worker     : num [1:2] 2405 2397
##  $ Productivity: output per hour worked: num [1:2] 10.9 11.4
##  $ GDP growth observations             : num [1:2] 7.02 6.78

Countries with lower average working hours often display higher productivity levels, suggesting a more efficient allocation of labour resources. Additionally, working hours shows an inverse effect on GDP growth where higher working hours doesn’t consistently translate to higher GDP growth. .

p <- ggplot(data, aes(
  x = `Annual working hours per worker`,
  y = `Productivity: output per hour worked`,
  colour = Entity,
  size = `GDP growth observations`
)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = 'Year: {frame_time}',
    x = "Annual Working Hours per Worker",
    y = "Productivity (GDP per Hour Worked)",
    caption = "Scatter plot showing working hours, productivity, and GDP growth (2015–2023)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  transition_time(Year) +
  ease_aes('linear')

animate(p, nframes = 100, fps = 10)

- There is a negative relationship between working hours and productivity, where countries with fewer working hours tend to have higher productivity levels and higher GDP growth.

- There is noticeable dispersion in the data, suggesting that other economic factors also influence productivity beyond working hours alone.

Conclusion

Analysis of the dataset indicates that countries with fewer annual working hours but higher productivity tend to experience stronger GDP growth, highlighting that efficient labour use drives economic performance more than sheer work volume. The scatter plot shows a general negative correlation between working hours and productivity, while GDP growth is higher in countries that balance moderate hours with high productivity. Variations across countries and years suggest that other factors—such as technology, policy, and investment—also influence economic outcomes. Overall, the findings support the conclusion that optimizing labour efficiency, rather than increasing work hours, is more strongly associated with positive GDP growth, aligning with the research question investigating how working hours and productivity relate to economic performance across countries from 2015–2023.
